content.text: How React Renders the HTML String
When React renders an element with
property directly. This bypasses React's virtual DOM reconciliation — React does not parse or diff the HTML string. The browser parses it and builds DOM nodes directly.
React does not allow children inside an element that uses

Not allowed — children and dangerouslySetInnerHTML are mutually exclusive
Cross-site scripting (XSS) occurs when an attacker injects executable script into HTML that renders in another user's browser. Even an image tag can execute JavaScript via the
attribute. Any user-controlled string rendered with
Never pass untrusted user input to
without sanitisation. 'Untrusted' includes any string from user input, a database, an API response, or URL parameters — even if your own system stored it originally.

DOMPurify is the industry-standard sanitiser for untrusted HTML in the browser. It parses the HTML string, removes dangerous tags and attributes, and returns a safe string. Always sanitise just before passing to
— not earlier, where the result might be re-combined with unsafe content.
DOMPurify requires the DOM to run — it is a browser-only library by default. For Node.js / SSR, use
to the new string. Memoize the string with
when the source data has not changed to avoid redundant work.
Renders via React elements — no innerHTML
No raw HTML string — no XSS surface
Full React control, no security concern
The structured JSON approach eliminates XSS entirely. Editors like TipTap and ProseMirror serialise to JSON; your renderer converts it to React elements without ever producing a raw HTML string.
to an inline HTML string in the server response. Sanitise on both sides — or store pre-sanitised HTML — to prevent a mismatch on hydration.

. React's synthetic event system only wraps elements in the React tree. DOM elements created by innerHTML are outside the React tree and require native
HTML authored by admins in a controlled CMS where the input is already sanitised at write time and stored in that form.
HTML produced entirely on the server by your own code (e.g., a PDF library that outputs HTML) with no user-controlled segments.
Libraries like Prism or highlight.js return highlighted HTML from source code strings — the source is code, not user narrative, and the output is sanitised by the library.
Rendering a pre-approved email template for preview, where the template is controlled by your system and validated before storage.

In every case above, the HTML source is controlled by your system, not by end users. Even then, applying DOMPurify as a defence-in-depth layer adds negligible overhead and protects against content-injection bugs elsewhere in your pipeline.

dangerouslySetInnerHTML sets a DOM element's innerHTML directly, bypassing React's virtual DOM. Its name is a deliberate warning: rendering untrusted HTML without sanitisation is a critical XSS vulnerability. Always sanitise with DOMPurify or an equivalent before passing to __html. Prefer structured document formats over raw HTML strings wherever possible. React event handlers do not work inside injected HTML.

DOMPurify allows you to declare an explicit allowlist of HTML tags and attributes. The tighter the allowlist, the smaller the attack surface. For user comments, a short list of formatting tags is usually sufficient. For a rich-text article body, you may need to permit more structural elements.
Passing no config object uses DOMPurify's defaults, which are fairly permissive. For user-generated content, always define an explicit allowlist — deny-by-default is safer than allow-by-default.

and contains links that should open in new tabs, React does not insert the security attributes automatically. Use a

Content Security Policy (CSP) is a browser mechanism that restricts which scripts, styles, and other resources a page can load. A properly configured CSP provides a critical second line of defence against XSS, even if sanitisation has a gap. Set the
header on your server with a restrictive

directive, inline scripts injected via XSS are blocked by the browser before they execute, even if the sanitiser missed them. CSP alone is not sufficient — it does not prevent data exfiltration via image or fetch requests — but it prevents JavaScript execution, which is the most common attack goal.
TypeScript: Enforcing Sanitisation at the Type Level
. Declaring a wrapper type that requires a sanitised value is a useful convention:
This pattern uses a TypeScript nominal type (a branded string) to make the sanitisation call mandatory. The brand has zero runtime cost — it is purely a type-level constraint.
requires checking the actual DOM output, not React's virtual tree. React Testing Library queries the real DOM, so you can assert on injected content directly.
was removed by DOMPurify. Including XSS-specific tests in your component suite makes the security guarantee explicit and prevents regressions if the sanitisation layer is accidentally removed.
When a component re-renders with a new HTML string, React updates the element's

without diffing the old DOM tree. Any DOM state that was set imperatively inside the injected HTML (focused elements, scroll position, input values) is lost on each update because the content is replaced entirely.

This behaviour is fundamentally different from React's normal rendering, where the reconciler preserves DOM nodes that did not change. Be aware of this when injecting HTML that contains interactive elements.
Code Highlighting: A Legitimate Use Case
Syntax highlighting libraries like Prism and highlight.js parse source code strings and return an HTML string with
elements carrying CSS classes for tokens. This is a legitimate use of
because the HTML is produced by the library from your own source code — not from user input.
Note that even for library-generated HTML, running DOMPurify adds negligible overhead and protects against supply-chain compromises where a library version might introduce unsafe output.
Nesting React Components in Injected HTML

HTML is not supported — the injected content is outside the React tree and React cannot manage it. If you need interactive components inside rich text, use a structured renderer that maps document nodes to React components (portals or custom renderers).
This approach keeps the entire UI tree in React, so event handlers, state, context, and refs all work normally on the rendered elements.

Was there a structured format (Markdown, JSON rich text) that would have avoided the need for raw HTML?

Practice: install DOMPurify in a React project and wrap every existing use of dangerouslySetInnerHTML in a sanitisation call. Then identify at least one place where you could replace raw HTML with a structured alternative.
